title: practical english usage - Part 270
content:
When  the holiday is over, we  may say: or Have a good/nice holiday (AmE  ... vacation),  Have a good/nice time.

Before somebody starts a holiday, we  may say: 10 holidays When  people return home, their friends or family may say Welcome back/home.

or dangerous events.

sometimes just Enjoy!). Good luck! is used before examinations or other difficult might say Have a good time! or Enjoy yourself! (especially in American English If somebody is leaving for an evening out or some kind of pleasant event, people How was the journey/trip/flight?

Did you have a good journey/trip/flight?

After a journey (for example when we  meet people at the airport or station), Safe journey home. (BrE) Have a good/nice trip. Have a good journey. (BrE) Common ways of wishing people a good journey are: 9 journeys,      etc ‘ You're going deaf' ‘ I beg your pardon?

‘ Seeyou tomorrow.' ‘ What? ‘ Seeyou tomorrow.' ‘ Mike's on the phone.' ‘ Sorry? ‘ I said Mike's on the phone.' What? (informal, not very polite), (I beg your) pardon? or Pardon me? (AmE).

If people do not hear or understand what is said, they may say Sorry? (BrE), 8  asking     people     to  repeat I beg your pardon. I didn't realise this was your seat.

I beg your pardon is a more formal way of saying 'Sorry'.

Americans also use Excuse me to apologise after disturbing somebody.

Excuse me, could you tell me the way to the station?

Excuse me. Could I get past? Oh,  sorry, did I step on your foot?

and Sorry after doing so. Compare: British people say Excuse me before interrupting or disturbing somebody, 7 apologies in a restaurant. We   do not normally say Excuse me, sir!madam (►  326.2 b).

Excuse me! is commonly used to attract somebody’ s attention, or to call a waiter 6 getting      people's      attention ‘ Nice day, isn't it? ‘ Lovely/ British people often begin polite conversations by talking about the weather.

vocabulary •  329   'social language’ There you go is also possible in this situation, especially in AmE.                          -+ 'Have you got a map of London? 'I think so. Yes, here you are.' "Thanks.' people’ s attention to the fact that we  are passing something to them.

things. We   sometimes say Here you are, especially when we  want to attract We   do not have an expression which  is automatically used when we  hand over 18  handing      over    things not Could you lend me a pen? (     Please lend me a pen;) We   normally ask for things by using yes/no questions. (►  310).

17 asking     for   things Note that thank you can be used for accepting as well as refusing.

No, thank you; Thanks, I'd love some; I'd love to; That's very nice! kind of you.

Can/May /.. .F or Shall I...? (mainly BrE). Typical replies are Yes, please; formal). Offers to do things for people can begin Would you like me to ...?, Offers often begin Would you like.. .For Can/May I get/offer you...? (more 16 offers     and   replies Thank you very/so much.  That was a wonderful evening.

It is normal to thank people for hospitality at the moment of leaving their places.

Sorry. I'm afraid I'm not free.

Thanks, that would be great, (informal) Thank you very/so much.  That would be very nice/lovely. (formal) Possible replies: Would you like to ...?

Invitations often begin: 15  invitations        and   visits deepest sympathy.

are / was very/terribly/extremely sorry to hear about... and Please accept my Common formulae in letters of sympathy (for example on somebody’ s death) 14 sympathy common expressions are X  sends his/her best wishes/regards, etc, X says hello.

Remember me to X, Say hello to X for me. When  the wishes are passed on, Typical expressions are Give my best wishes /regards /greetings/love to X, 13 sending      good    wishes Here's to Lily! Here's to the new job!  Here's to the happy couple!

(such as a birthday, a wedding or a promotion), we  often say Here's to...!

health! (AmE  also To your health). When  we  drink to celebrate an occasion glasses and say something. Common expressions are Cheers! (BrE) and Your When  people begin drinking alcoholic drinks socially, they often raise their 12 drinking For the names of meals, ►    330.
